questions.title: Behavioral Guidelines Persistence
questions.text: Where should you define behavioral guidelines (enthusiastic tone, explain reasoning, ask clarifying questions) to persist reliably across all user interactions?

options.A: In the first assistant message, instructing Claude to follow these guidelines going forward
options.B: In the system prompt
options.C: In environmental variables that your application passes to the API client
options.D: Prepended to each user message

questions.explanation: Why B: The system prompt is specifically designed for persistent behavioral instructions that apply to every interaction. Environmental variables have no effect on model behavior.

questions.title: Contradictory User Preferences
questions.text: User stated "very low risk tolerance" but later says "I want to maximize returns." Asks "What should I invest in?" Which approach best ensures the recommendation aligns with actual priority?

options.A: Proceed with the most recently stated preference
options.B: Provide separate recommendations for both scenarios without clarifying which the user prefers
options.C: Recommend a balanced portfolio without explicitly addressing the conflict
options.D: Surface the contradiction and ask the user to clarify which matters more—capital preservation or growth potential—before recommending

questions.explanation: Why D: When preferences directly contradict each other, the correct approach is to surface the conflict and ask for clarification rather than silently picking one or splitting the difference.

questions.title: Conversation Memory Not Working
questions.text: Claude asks what genres a user enjoys even though they said "I love jazz" two messages earlier. Most likely cause?

options.A: Claude requires a vector database connection to maintain conversation memory
options.B: Your application isn't including prior messages in the messages array
options.C: The Claude API requires a session_id parameter that you haven't configured
options.D: The model's context window has been exceeded by the conversation length

questions.explanation: Why B: Claude has no inherent memory. The application must include all prior conversation turns in the messages array with each API request.

questions.title: System Prompt Update Breaks Existing Conversations
questions.text: Updated system prompt improves quality for new users but causes contradictions and style changes for long-running multi-session users. Best approach?

options.A: Add instructions to the new prompt directing the assistant to maintain consistency with prior statements
options.B: Version system prompts and associate each conversation with the prompt version under which it started, applying updates only to new conversations
options.C: Add a transition message when sessions resume explaining behavior may differ
options.D: Regenerate summaries of existing conversations using the new prompt

questions.explanation: Why B: Versioning prompts per conversation preserves consistency for existing users while allowing improvements for new ones. Regenerating histories (D) risks altering the meaning of past exchanges.

questions.title: Implicit Expertise Signals
questions.text: Fitness assistant defaults to overly detailed responses when users don't explicitly state experience level, despite correct behavior for explicit declarations. Which system prompt change addresses this?

options.A: Implement a pre-conversation intake asking users to rate their experience level
options.B: Replace most conditionals with a general principle: "Adapt explanation depth to match user expertise, mirroring their terminology." Keep only the safety-critical conditional about injury consultations
options.C: Add more conditional branches for additional expertise signals
options.D: Add an explicit instruction for the model to ask a clarifying question about experience level when unclear

questions.explanation: Why B: A general principle allows the model to apply judgment to implicit cues (like technical terminology). More conditionals (C) fail on cases not explicitly covered.

questions.title: Long Conversation Context Management (Book Club, 85K tokens)
questions.text: Users ask about conclusions from earlier sessions but get generic responses. Discussions build on previous conclusions. Most effective approach?

options.A: Implement rolling window truncation to keep only the most recent 25,000 tokens
options.B: Use semantic embedding to index the full conversation history and retrieve only relevant past exchanges
options.C: Implement progressive summarization where older conversation blocks are replaced with concise summaries that explicitly extract key conclusions, decisions, and recurring themes, while keeping recent exchanges verbatim
options.D: Add structured XML tags to mark significant discussion conclusions throughout the conversation history

questions.explanation: Why C: Progressive summarization explicitly preserves key conclusions (the valuable signal) while freeing up context space. Simple truncation (A) loses the conclusions.

questions.title: 40% Abandonment Rate from Too Many Clarifying Questions
questions.text: Assistant asks 3 clarifying questions at once; 40% of users abandon. Best approach to reduce friction while handling ambiguity?

options.A: Create a lookup table of common request patterns with predefined default interpretations
options.B: Add a preprocessing step using a smaller model to classify request ambiguity on a 1–5 scale
options.C: Limit the assistant to one clarifying question per turn
options.D: Modify the system prompt to instruct the assistant to make reasonable assumptions from available context, state those assumptions explicitly, and offer to adjust if the interpretation is wrong

questions.explanation: Why D: Making transparent assumptions and inviting correction reduces friction dramatically while still producing useful responses. Over-engineering (B) adds latency and complexity.

questions.title: Story Writing: Context Limits, Persistent vs. Ephemeral Content
questions.text: After 40+ turns story assistant "forgets" character traits. Persistent elements (world rules, character backgrounds) vs. ephemeral brainstorming. Best approach?

options.A: Apply a sliding-window keeping only the most recent 25 turns
options.B: Summarize the entire conversation history every 20 turns
options.C: Separate persistent story elements into a retained "story bible" section at context start, applying trimming or summarization only to brainstorming discussion
options.D: Store all history in a vector database and retrieve semantically similar passages

questions.explanation: Why C: This directly addresses the two-category problem. The story bible stays in context permanently; only ephemeral brainstorming is compressed.

questions.title: Research Assistant Loses Precision After 60K Tokens
questions.text: Precision-dependent questions (exact p-values, sample sizes) become hedged/inaccurate after summarization. Most effective architectural change?

options.A: Implement retrieval that re-injects relevant paper sections when questions suggest numerical details are needed
options.B: Maintain a structured database of key facts extracted from each paper (sample sizes, statistics, methods) and retrieve relevant entries into context when precision-dependent questions are detected
options.C: Use a separate Claude call with explicit instructions to generate higher-fidelity summaries
options.D: Keep source text from methodology and results sections permanently in context

questions.explanation: Why B: A structured fact database directly solves the precision problem—exact numbers are stored and retrieved, bypassing the lossy summarization step.

questions.title: Real Estate Assistant Ignores Updated Preferences
questions.text: Users update budget/property type mid-conversation but assistant references original preferences. Context at only 35%. Most reliable solution?

options.A: Include few-shot examples showing the assistant correctly acknowledging and applying preference changes
options.B: Add system prompt instructions emphasizing most recently stated preferences over earlier ones
options.C: Maintain a structured state object with current preferences, update it on changes, and include it in each request
options.D: Implement conversation pruning to remove turns containing outdated preferences

questions.explanation: Why C: A managed state object explicitly tracks the current value. This is more reliable than hoping the model notices updates in a long conversation (B) or pruning history (D).

questions.title: Event Planning: 4.2 Average Clarifying Questions, 35% Abandonment
questions.text: Reducing questions causes poor recommendations; keeping them causes abandonment. Best trade-off?

options.A: Configure the assistant to consolidate all clarifying questions into a single compound question
options.B: Instruct the assistant to state explicit assumptions based on conversation history, proceed with recommendations while inviting corrections, and reserve clarifying questions only for irreversible actions like confirming bookings
options.C: Implement a structured intake form that collects all required parameters upfront
options.D: Configure the assistant to proceed with reasonable defaults without explicitly stating these assumptions

questions.explanation: Why B: This threads the needle perfectly—reduce questions while remaining transparent and correctable. Reserving questions for irreversible actions is the right risk-calibrated approach.

questions.title: Ambiguous First Message: "Set up my focus music"
questions.text: Could mean configure preferences, create playlist, or play immediately. Best approach?

options.A: Create a new "Focus" playlist with curated tracks and notify the user it's ready
options.B: Start preference configuration by asking about genres, tempo, and artists
options.C: Ask one clarifying question about action type: play now or configure for later
options.D: Play popular focus tracks immediately and let the user redirect if needed

questions.explanation: Why C: The fundamental ambiguity is between immediate playback and setup—one targeted question resolves this cleanly without launching into a lengthy preference flow.

questions.title: 78K Token Dinner Planning: Critical Safety Info + General Chat
questions.text: Session contains: shellfish allergy, recipe measurements, user-defined terms, and general back-and-forth. Best context management?

options.A: Implement a sliding window retaining only the most recent 20,000 tokens
options.B: Extract critical structured data (allergies, serving counts, user-defined terms) into a compact reference section, summarize general discussion, and retain recent exchanges verbatim
options.C: Store the full conversation externally and use semantic search
options.D: Summarize the entire conversation history into a concise summary

questions.explanation: Why B: The allergies and user-defined terms must never be lost—they're safety-critical. This approach treats different content types differently, which is the right architecture.

questions.title: Latency and Costs Rise After 50+ Conversation Turns
questions.text: PRIMARY cause of increasing API latency and costs?

options.A: The model generates progressively longer responses as it accumulates more context
options.B: The entire conversation history is included with each API request, so input tokens grow with every turn
options.C: The model builds an internal profile of the user's conversation patterns
options.D: Database operations for retrieving and storing conversation history slow down

questions.explanation: Why B: Claude is stateless. Every request re-sends the full conversation history. As turns accumulate, input tokens grow linearly, directly increasing both cost and latency.

questions.title: Repetitive Response Openers ("Certainly!", "I'd be happy to help!")
questions.text: Users report responses feel repetitive across turns. Most effective approach?

options.A: Append a partial assistant message with a direct response opening that the model will continue from
options.B: Implement post-processing to detect and strip common greeting phrases from response beginnings
options.C: Lower the temperature parameter
options.D: Add system prompt instructions specifying phrases to avoid, such as "Never begin responses with 'Certainly' or similar affirmations"

questions.explanation: Why D: System prompt instructions address the root cause. Post-processing (B) is brittle and adds latency; lowering temperature (C) doesn't control phrasing choices.

questions.title: Tool Ordering: extract_metadata Before Enrichment
questions.text: Claude sometimes calls lookup_citations before extract_metadata. Most effective fix?

options.A: Set tool_choice to "any" combined with system prompt instructions
options.B: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} and process the enrichment requests in subsequent turns after receiving the extracted metadata
options.C: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} for every API call in the pipeline
options.D: Set tool_choice to "auto" and reorder tool definitions

questions.explanation: Why B: Option C forces extract_metadata on EVERY call, meaning enrichment tools can never run. Option B correctly sequences: force extraction in turn 1, then allow enrichment in subsequent turns.

questions.title: property_type Enum Failing for Unusual Types
questions.text: 8% of extractions fail schema validation. New property types keep appearing. Best long-term solution?

options.A: Continuously expand the enum to include newly observed property types
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating how to map unexpected types to the closest existing enum value
options.C: Add an "other" value to your enum with a separate property_type_detail string field for specifics when "other" is selected
options.D: Change property_type from an enum to a free-form string and implement normalization in post-processing

questions.explanation: Why C: This maintains the structure and queryability of an enum while gracefully accommodating any future edge case through the detail field.

questions.title: Standard Reports vs. Urgent Exception Reports
questions.text: Minimize costs while meeting latency requirements. Architecture?

options.A: Submit all documents to the Batch API with custom_ids for tracking
options.B: Route standard reports to the Batch API for 50% cost savings, and route urgent exception reports to the real-time Messages API
options.C: Queue all documents and submit hourly batches
options.D: Submit all documents to the real-time Messages API

questions.explanation: Why B: The correct architecture matches the tool to the requirement. Batch = cost savings for non-urgent. Real-time = required for the 30-min SLA.

questions.title: Batch Processing Strategy: 30-Hour SLA
questions.text: Documents arrive continuously. Which batching frequency satisfies 99.9% reliability within 30 hours?

options.A: Submit a single batch at end of day
options.B: Submit batches every 6 hours (6 + 24 = 30 hrs — no buffer)
options.C: Use the real-time API for all documents instead of batch processing
options.D: Submit batches every 4 hours containing documents from that window

questions.explanation: Why D: Max wait in queue = 4 hrs. Max processing = 24 hrs. Total = 28 hrs, leaving a 2-hour buffer within the 30-hour SLA while still getting the 50% batch discount.

questions.title: Inconsistent Skill Extraction (skills: string[])
questions.text: Issues: compound phrases sometimes split/kept, implied skills appear, wildly different array lengths. Current prompt: "Extract all skills mentioned." Most effective improvement?

options.A: Enrich schema to {skill: string, confidence: float, source_quote: string}[]
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating compound phrase handling, explicit mention criteria, and appropriate entry granularity
options.C: Add post-extraction normalization that maps skills to a canonical taxonomy
options.D: Add constraints: "Extract 10-20 skills maximum, one skill per entry, only explicitly named skills"

questions.explanation: Why B: All three issues are judgment calls (what counts as one skill, what counts as explicit, what granularity is appropriate). Few-shot examples demonstrate the exact behavior rather than imposing potentially incorrect rules.

questions.title: Accuracy Drops for Long Documents
questions.text: Tool definitions are 2,500 tokens. Documents 150K → 98% accuracy; 175-190K → 71%. Context window is 200K. Most likely cause?

options.A: The model distributes attention proportionally, causing less focus on content near the end
options.B: Schemas exceeding 8-10 fields increase decision complexity
options.C: Tool definitions consume input context tokens. Combined with system prompts and document content, the total approaches the context limit, degrading end-of-document processing
options.D: Very long documents exceed the model's effective attention span

questions.explanation: Why C: 2,500 (tool def) + ~2,000 (system prompt) + 175,000-190,000 (document) ≈ 180-195K, approaching the 200K limit. Content near the end of long documents gets cut off or degraded.

questions.title: Plausible but Incorrect Values for Missing Fields
questions.text: Event extraction: model outputs "500" for attendee_count when article contains no attendance information. Most effective fix?

options.A: Add a post-processing step using a second LLM call to verify each extracted value exists in the source document
options.B: Upgrade to a more capable model tier
options.C: Add prompt instructions to return null for any field where information is not directly stated in the source
options.D: Make all schema fields required (non-nullable)

questions.explanation: Why C: This directly addresses the root cause—the model needs explicit permission and instruction to return null rather than hallucinate plausible values.

questions.title: Invoice Extraction: Line Items Sum Mismatch
questions.text: Downstream systems reject mismatched records. Most effective approach?

options.A: Add a "calculated_total" field where the model sums extracted line items alongside a "stated_total" field. Flag records for human review when values differ
options.B: Add few-shot examples demonstrating invoices where extracted line items sum correctly
options.C: Implement post-processing that automatically adjusts line item amounts proportionally when their sum doesn't match the stated total
options.D: Extract line items and totals independently, then use a separate validation model

questions.explanation: Why A: This captures both values and surfaces discrepancies for human judgment. Auto-adjusting proportionally (C) silently corrupts data—the discrepancy might be a real OCR error or a real model error that needs human review.

questions.title: context_length_exceeded for 300 Documents
questions.text: Most cost-effective approach to process only the failures?

options.A: Reprocess the entire batch with prompt caching enabled
options.B: Resubmit only the 300 failed documents after chunking them into smaller pieces, then combine the partial extractions
options.C: Increase the max_tokens parameter for the 300 failed documents and resubmit in a new batch
options.D: Resubmit the entire 10,000 document batch using a model tier with a larger context window

questions.explanation: Why B: context_length_exceeded means the INPUT is too long—max_tokens (C) controls output length, not input. Chunking the documents that are too large is the correct fix, and reprocessing only the 300 failures is the most cost-efficient approach.

questions.title: Retry-with-Error-Feedback: LEAST Helped by Retries
questions.text: For which failure would additional retries be LEAST effective?

options.A: The model extracts "et al." for co-authors when the full list exists only in an external document not in the input
options.B: The model extracts citation counts as locale-formatted strings ("1,234") when the schema requires integers
options.C: The model extracts dates as ISO 8601 datetime strings when the schema requires only the date portion
options.D: The model extracts keywords as a nested object when the schema requires a flat array of strings

questions.explanation: Why A: B, C, and D are all format/structure errors that can be fixed by error feedback on retry. A is fundamentally different—the data doesn't exist in the input at all. No amount of retrying can produce information that isn't there.

questions.title: 12% Semantic Validation Failure
questions.text: JSON syntax never fails. Most effective approach to reduce manual review?

options.A: Implement post-processing logic that automatically corrects common errors
options.B: Retry the extraction up to 3 times when validation fails, accepting first result that passes
options.C: When validation fails, make a follow-up request with the document, extraction, and validation errors for model correction
options.D: Add stricter schema constraints with detailed field descriptions to prevent invalid values initially

questions.explanation: Why C: Giving the model the full context (document + failed extraction + specific errors) lets it understand exactly what went wrong and fix it precisely. Stricter constraints (D) help with output format but not semantic validity (sums, ID formats).

questions.title: Contract Amendments: Original vs. Amendment
questions.text: "30-day payment terms" vs "45 days" in Amendment 1. Most effective improvement?

options.A: Post-extraction validation using pattern matching to flag amendments for manual review
options.B: Add prompt instructions to always extract the most recent amendment value and ignore superseded original terms
options.C: Redesign the schema so amended fields capture multiple values, each with source location and effective date
options.D: Preprocess documents with a classifier that identifies and removes superseded sections

questions.explanation: Why C: Legal contracts often need both values preserved with provenance. Discarding original terms (B, D) loses legally important history. The schema redesign is the most complete and accurate solution.

questions.title: Inconsistent "Materials" Field Output
questions.text: "cotton blend" vs "Cotton/Polyester mix" vs field omitted when present. Most effective fix?

options.A: Add few-shot examples showing 2-3 complete input-output pairs with standardized material description formats
options.B: Make the "materials" field required instead of optional to force the model to always extract a value
options.C: Switch to a more capable model tier
options.D: Set temperature to 0 to eliminate randomness and ensure deterministic outputs

questions.explanation: Why A: Temperature 0 reduces variation but doesn't tell the model WHICH format to use. Few-shot examples directly demonstrate the desired output format.

questions.title: High-Confidence Extractions Still Have 12% Error Rate
questions.text: Errors from comparison tables, appendices, ambiguous phrasing. Need sustainable strategy to catch errors AND measure improvement over time.

options.A: Add a verification pass that re-extracts from each high-confidence document, flagging where two attempts differ
options.B: Lower the confidence threshold from 85% to 70%
options.C: Implement heuristic rules that flag documents containing comparison tables or appendices for review regardless of confidence score
options.D: Implement stratified random sampling reviewing a fixed percentage of high-confidence extractions weekly, enabling error rate measurement and novel pattern detection

questions.explanation: Why D: This is the only approach that enables ongoing error rate measurement AND discovers novel failure patterns. Heuristics (C) only catch known patterns.

questions.title: Automating High-Confidence Extractions (97% Accuracy)
questions.text: System has 100% human review for 3 months. Before deploying automation, what validation is most critical?

options.A: Analyze accuracy by document type and field to verify high-confidence extractions perform consistently across all segments, not just in aggregate
options.B: Verify that 97% accuracy meets requirements for all downstream systems that consume the extracted data
options.C: Compare accuracy at different confidence thresholds (85%, 90%, 95%) to find the optimal cutoff
options.D: Run a two-week pilot routing 25% of high-confidence extractions directly to downstream systems

questions.explanation: Why A: 97% aggregate accuracy can hide 70% accuracy on specific document types or fields. Segment-level analysis before automation prevents deploying a system that silently fails on particular cases.

questions.title: Customer Demands Human Agent NOW
questions.text: Customer says: "I want to talk to a real person NOW." Agent has not called any tools. What should the agent do?

options.A: Immediately call escalate_to_human with the conversation history
options.B: Acknowledge the frustration and ask one targeted question to understand the specific issue before escalating
options.C: First call get_customer and lookup_order to gather account context, then escalate to a human agent
options.D: Briefly explain what the agent can help with and offer to resolve the issue quickly

questions.explanation: Why A: The customer has issued an explicit, emphatic request for a human. Delaying that by gathering context first (C) adds more friction to an already frustrated customer.

questions.title: Designing Escalation Trigger Logic
questions.text: Which approach most reliably identifies cases that genuinely require human intervention?

options.A: Instruct the agent to escalate when the customer requests a human, when the issue requires policy exceptions, or when the agent cannot make meaningful progress
options.B: Implement sentiment analysis that monitors for frustration indicators and triggers when frustration score exceeds a threshold
options.C: Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool calls that fail to resolve the stated issue
options.D: Build a rules engine that maps specific issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions

questions.explanation: Why A: This covers the three genuine escalation conditions. Rules engines (D) are brittle and miss edge cases; sentiment analysis (B) can misfire or miss real needs.

questions.title: process_refund Technical vs. Business Errors
questions.text: Agent wastes 3-4 turns retrying permanent business errors. Both return plain text. Most effective fix?

options.A: Implement automatic retry logic at the tool level for technical errors only
options.B: Add a check_refund_eligibility tool that must be called before process_refund
options.C: Return structured error responses with retriable: false for business errors and a customer-friendly explanation for Claude to use
options.D: Add few-shot examples showing how to distinguish retriable from non-retriable errors

questions.explanation: Why C: Structured error metadata directly tells the agent whether to retry or not. This eliminates wasted turns without restructuring the entire workflow (B).

questions.title: Frustrated Customer Wants Human, Return Is Straightforward
questions.text: "I've been going back and forth for days. I just want to speak to someone." Return is within policy and eligible for immediate processing.

options.A: Process the refund via process_refund to resolve the underlying issue, then inform them it's complete
options.B: Call escalate_to_human immediately to honor the customer's request
options.C: Ask what specifically hasn't worked in previous attempts before deciding
options.D: Acknowledge frustration, inform them this is resolvable now, and offer to complete it or escalate

questions.explanation: Why D: The customer's real goal is resolution. Acknowledging their frustration, showing the problem is solvable, and giving them the choice respects their experience while delivering maximum value.

questions.title: lookup_order Called Multiple Times; Context Dominated
questions.text: 40+ fields per response, most of context is tool output. Customer mentions 2 more orders. Most effective before additional lookups?

options.A: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing
options.B: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, status) from each existing order response, removing verbose details
options.C: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order's key details, replacing structured responses with prose
options.D: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output context

questions.explanation: Why B: Extracting only the fields relevant to the current task (returns) preserves the data needed while dramatically reducing context usage. Natural language summaries (C) lose structured data needed for subsequent tool calls.

questions.title: Mid-Process Escalation: Billing Dispute
questions.text: Agent has gathered context via get_customer and lookup_order but issue exceeds authorization limit. How to handle the escalation?

options.A: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call escalate_to_human with a reference ID
options.B: Call escalate_to_human passing only the customer's original message
options.C: Attempt the refund with process_refund anyway, escalating only if the system rejects the transaction
options.D: Compile a structured handoff with customer details, order info, and the identified issue before calling escalate_to_human

questions.explanation: Why D: A structured, synthesized summary gives the human agent exactly what they need for effective resolution—more useful than a raw reference ID (A) or just the original message (B).

questions.title: Compliance: Refunds >$500 Must Always Escalate
questions.text: Despite clear system prompt instructions, 3% failure rate persists. How to achieve guaranteed compliance?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the prompt showing correct escalation behavior at various refund amounts
options.B: Strengthen the system prompt with emphatic language: "CRITICAL POLICY: Refunds over $500 MUST trigger human escalation. NEVER process these directly."
options.C: Modify the refund tool to return an error with message "Amount exceeds policy limit—please escalate" when threshold is exceeded
options.D: Implement a hook to intercept tool calls; when the refund process amount exceeds $500, block it and invoke human escalation

questions.explanation: Why D: If the system prompt already fails 3% of the time, stronger wording (B) won't help. A hook that intercepts and blocks the tool call at the infrastructure level is the only approach that guarantees enforcement regardless of model behavior.

questions.title: Agent Asks for Customer's Name Again
questions.text: Multi-step identity verification; agent re-asks for name after the third verification question. Most likely cause?

options.A: Claude's memory retention is limited to two conversational turns by default
options.B: The conversation history isn't being passed in subsequent API requests
options.C: The prompt lacks instructions telling Claude to remember information across multiple exchanges
options.D: The verification tool is clearing the agent's internal state after each successful validation step

questions.explanation: Why B: Claude is stateless. If prior turns aren't included in the messages array, the model has no access to what was established earlier in the conversation.

questions.title: MCP Tool lookup_order: Error Communication Pattern
questions.text: Backend sometimes returns "Order not found" or temporary database failures. What is the correct pattern?

options.A: Log the error server-side and return an empty result to avoid confusing the model
options.B: Return a success response with a "status" field indicating the error type
options.C: Return the error message in the tool result content with the isError flag set to true
options.D: Throw an exception from the tool handler so the agent framework can catch and log it

questions.explanation: Why C: This is the correct MCP protocol for tool errors. The agent receives structured information about the failure and can decide how to respond appropriately.

questions.title: process_refund Timeout With Context
questions.text: Agent retrieved customer info and order details but process_refund times out. What approach best balances resolution with error handling?

options.A: Explain the billing, confirm refund eligibility, acknowledge the system issue preventing immediate processing, and offer escalation or retry later
options.B: Implement automatic retries with exponential backoff for process_refund, keeping the conversation open until the refund is successfully processed
options.C: Confirm the refund will be processed and close the conversation
options.D: Escalate immediately to a human agent since the refund action cannot be completed

questions.explanation: Why A: The agent provides maximum value with available information, is transparent about the technical limitation, and gives the customer actionable next steps. Blocking on retries (B) holds the customer hostage to an uncertain backend recovery.

questions.title: Escalation Handoff After 25+ Turns: $847 Refund
questions.text: Billing dispute resolved: duplicate charges from payment gateway timeout. Human agent won't have transcript access. What context to pass?

options.A: A structured summary: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action
options.B: The customer's original complaint verbatim plus tool result excerpts showing duplicate transactions
options.C: Your diagnosis and the refund amount only
options.D: The complete conversation transcript with all tool results

questions.explanation: Why A: A synthesized structured summary is what the human agent needs to act quickly. The complete transcript (D) is too much; only the diagnosis (C) is too little.

questions.title: Customer Raises 3 Issues in One Session
questions.text: At turn 48, customer asks about their refund from turns 1–15. Strategy to maintain all issues?

options.A: Rely on MCP tools to re-fetch relevant information on demand when the customer references earlier issues
options.B: Implement sliding window context that retains the most recent 30 turns
options.C: Extract and persist structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer
options.D: Summarize earlier turns into a narrative description

questions.explanation: Why C: A structured data layer persists the key facts from all three issues independently of the conversation window, so any issue can be referenced accurately at any point in the session.

questions.title: Customer Returns 4 Hours Later with Stale Tool Data
questions.text: Previous session has lookup_order results with "Status: PENDING, Expected resolution: 24-48 hours." Agent references this stale data even after fresh tool calls return different information. Most reliable approach?

options.A: Resume with full history and add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to always prefer the most recent tool results when multiple calls to the same tool exist
options.B: Resume with full history and configure the agent to automatically re-call all previously-used tools at session start
options.C: Resume with full history but filter out previous tool_result messages before resuming
options.D: Start a new session, inject a structured summary of the previous interaction (issue type, actions taken, resolution status), then make fresh tool calls before engaging

questions.explanation: Why D: Starting fresh with a summary and fresh data guarantees no stale results can influence responses. Option A still leaves stale data in context and relies on the model to correctly prioritize the newer results.

questions.title: Inconsistent Error Handling Due to Uniform Errors
questions.text: MCP tool returns {"isError": true, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Operation failed"}]} for all errors. Agent retries incorrectly, escalates prematurely, or asks for clarification inappropriately. Most effective improvement?

options.A: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns
options.C: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: include errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and a description of what caused the failure
options.D: Create an analyze_error MCP tool the agent calls after any failure

questions.explanation: Why C: Structured error metadata gives the agent exactly the information it needs to choose the right response strategy for each type of failure without any ambiguity.

questions.title: Agentic Loop Chooses Next Tool After lookup_order
questions.text: After receiving order details showing purchase 45 days ago, how does the agentic loop determine whether to call process_refund or escalate_to_human?

options.A: The orchestration layer automatically routes to the next tool based on the order's status field
options.B: The agent executes the remaining steps in a tool sequence planned at the start of the request
options.C: The agent follows a pre-configured decision tree mapping order attributes to specific tool calls
options.D: The order details are added to the conversation and the model reasons about which action to take

questions.explanation: Why D: This is how Claude's agentic loops work. Tool results become part of the conversation context as tool_result messages, and the model uses those results plus the system prompt policy to reason about the next step.

questions.title: MCP readOnlyHint vs. destructiveHint Annotations
questions.text: Team proposes bypassing confirmation for tools annotated readOnlyHint: true. What should guide this decision?

options.A: Implement a verification layer testing each tool's actual behavior before adding it to an allowlist
options.B: MCP servers run as local processes, so tools from properly initialized connections can be trusted regardless of vendor
options.C: Treat tool annotations as untrusted metadata unless the server itself is trusted. Base confirmation requirements on your assessment of each vendor's trustworthiness, not on their self-reported annotations
options.D: Read-only annotations indicate intended behavior and are reliable for tools that perform GET requests

questions.explanation: Why C: A server can annotate any destructive tool as read-only. Annotations are self-reported metadata, not independently verifiable. Trust the vendor, not the annotation.

questions.title: MCP Server vs. Custom Tool for Weather API
questions.text: Primary advantage of the MCP approach when multiple AI applications need weather data?

options.A: Any MCP-compatible client can connect to your weather server without writing custom integration code
options.B: MCP servers process tool calls faster than custom implementations because they use an optimized binary protocol
options.C: MCP provides built-in retry logic that automatically handles failed API calls with exponential backoff
options.D: MCP automatically handles authentication and rate limiting with the weather API, reducing backend implementation work

questions.explanation: Why A: The primary value of MCP is standardized reusability—build the integration once, and any MCP-compatible client (current or future) can use it. MCP does not automatically handle auth or rate limiting (D).

questions.title: post_content Confirmation Redesign
questions.text: Posts went to wrong accounts, scheduled at wrong times, or contained errors—all confirmed by users without catching mistakes. How to redesign?

options.A: Include the complete post text, target account, scheduled time, and platform in the confirmation request
options.B: Auto-approve routine posts and only require explicit confirmation for unusual patterns like posting to new accounts or large audiences
options.C: Add a mandatory waiting period before the confirm option becomes available
options.D: Require users to type a confirmation phrase instead of clicking a button

questions.explanation: Why A: Users were rubber-stamping confirmations because the confirmation didn't show them what they were approving. Making the full details visible in the confirmation surfaces the errors before they happen.

questions.title: search_documents Database Selection Parameter
questions.text: Three databases; users express naturally ("search the research database"). How to design the parameter?

options.A: A freeform string parameter where the backend uses semantic matching to determine which database(s) to search
options.B: No explicit parameter—search all three databases by default, then have the model filter results by source
options.C: An enum parameter with values ["research_papers", "internal_reports", "technical_specs"], requiring the model to map natural language to the appropriate value
options.D: A freeform string parameter with runtime validation that returns an error if the value doesn't match a known database

questions.explanation: Why C: Enum parameters are ideal here. The model reliably maps natural language to enum values, and the parameter prevents invalid values at the schema level.

questions.title: archive_file vs delete_file Tool Selection
questions.text: Both tools have minimal descriptions. Agent calls delete_file when company policy requires archiving backups. Which change most directly improves tool selection?

options.A: Implement server-side validation that rejects delete_file calls for files tagged as backups
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating that requests involving "backup" or "old" should use archive_file
options.C: Add a confirmation step that requires users to type "CONFIRM DELETE" before delete_file executes
options.D: Expand tool descriptions to clarify use cases, adding guidance like "Do not use for backup files" to delete_file and when to use archive_file

questions.explanation: Why D: The model makes tool selection decisions based on descriptions. Clear, accurate descriptions with explicit guidance about use cases directly solve the tool selection problem at the source.

questions.title: get_portfolio_value: Structured JSON vs. Text
questions.text: Primary advantage of using structured output with defined fields?

options.A: Structured JSON is processed deterministically by the model, significantly improving accuracy when extracting values
options.B: Structured JSON consumes significantly fewer tokens than natural language
options.C: The agent can reliably extract specific values without parsing free-form text, reducing errors in subsequent operations
options.D: JSON schemas automatically validate that the underlying API returned correct data before the agent processes it

questions.explanation: Why C: The primary advantage is reliable, error-free data access in subsequent agent steps. Free-form text requires error-prone parsing; structured fields are directly accessible.

questions.title: remove_team_member dry_run Bypassed
questions.text: Agent calls with dry_run=false directly, skipping the required preview+confirm workflow. Most reliable enforcement?

options.A: Add server-side validation that permits dry_run=false only when a dry_run=true call with identical parameters occurred within the past 60 seconds
options.B: Add detailed instructions and few-shot examples to the tool description
options.C: Replace with two tools: preview_remove_member returns impact details and a single-use confirmation token; execute_remove_member requires that token, binding execution to the specific previewed action
options.D: Annotate the tool as requiring confirmation and configure the orchestration layer to prompt the user for approval

questions.explanation: Why C: The single-use confirmation token structurally enforces the preview→confirm sequence. It is architecturally impossible to skip the preview, regardless of how the agent is prompted.

questions.title: delete_contact: Misidentified Records
questions.text: Current multi-step confirmation adds friction. Reduce error rate while maintaining efficiency?

options.A: Present matched records with differentiating fields and require single-click confirmation of the intended target before executing deletion
options.B: Deploy automated duplicate detection that identifies and merges probable duplicates
options.C: Implement soft-delete with a 30-day recovery window
options.D: Require users to supply the exact record ID from the CRM interface rather than using natural language references

questions.explanation: Why A: This directly addresses the root cause (misidentification from similar names) by surfacing differentiating information before confirming, while keeping the workflow to a single confirmation click.

questions.title: send_notification Timeouts
questions.text: Currently returns generic is_error: true, agents retry, causing duplicate notifications. How to modify error response?

options.A: Return is_error: true with a structured field retry_safe: true for timeouts
options.B: Return is_error: false with the original message content echoed back
options.C: Return is_error: true with a message encouraging retry
options.D: Return is_error: true with a message communicating uncertainty: "Timeout—status unknown. Message may have been sent. Avoid retry."

questions.explanation: Why D: The critical information is the delivery uncertainty and the instruction NOT to retry. Option A with retry_safe: true would cause the exact duplicate problem you're trying to prevent.

questions.title: Agentic loop control flow
questions.text: When implementing an agentic loop with the Claude API, which stop_reason values should cause the loop to execute tools and continue versus present the final response to the user?

options.A: Continue on end_turn; terminate on tool_use
options.B: Continue on tool_use; terminate on end_turn
options.C: Continue on both tool_use and max_tokens; terminate only on end_turn
options.D: Terminate after any tool call completes, regardless of stop_reason

questions.explanation: Why B: The agentic loop continues while stop_reason is tool_use (execute tools, append results, call again) and stops when stop_reason is end_turn. Reversing these (A) or stopping after every tool call (D) breaks standard agentic control flow.

questions.title: search_flights: 503 Service Unavailable
questions.text: Most effective way to handle this error in your tool implementation?

options.A: Return an error message in the tool result explaining the service is temporarily unavailable
options.B: Log the error internally and return an empty response, letting the model continue without the flight data
options.C: Return an empty flight list as if the search succeeded but found no matching flights
options.D: Automatically retry the request up to five times with exponential backoff before returning results

questions.explanation: Why A: Returning a clear error message (with isError: true) lets the agent understand the failure and communicate it to the user. An empty response (B) is ambiguous. An empty results list (C) misleads the agent. Auto-retrying 5 times (D) could add tens of seconds of latency to every 503 error.

questions.title: process_reimbursement: $500 Approval Threshold
questions.text: Which design ensures the threshold cannot be bypassed regardless of how the agent is prompted?

options.A: Provide two tools (auto_reimburse hard-coded to $500 and request_manager_approval) with system prompt instructions and audit logging
options.B: process_reimbursement accepts an approved_by_manager boolean; prompt instructs agent to only set this to true after confirming approval
options.C: process_reimbursement internally enforces the threshold: amounts ≤$500 auto-disbursed, amounts >$500 create a pending approval request and return a status indicating manager review is pending
options.D: Implement a PreToolUse hook that inspects the amount parameter and modifies the tool call to add requires_approval: true when threshold is exceeded

questions.explanation: Why C: The business rule is enforced at the tool implementation level, completely independent of the agent's behavior. The agent cannot bypass it regardless of how it is prompted.

questions.title: log_workout: Invalid Measurement Combinations
questions.text: Cardio exercises get "reps", strength exercises get "miles". Most effective fix?

options.A: Add enum constraints on measurement limiting values to "minutes", "miles", "reps", or "sets"
options.B: Split into log_cardio_workout (with duration_minutes or distance_miles parameters) and log_strength_workout (with reps and sets parameters)
options.C: Add explicit examples to the tool description showing valid combinations (e.g., "For running: use minutes or miles. For push-ups: use reps")
options.D: Implement server-side validation returning descriptive errors for invalid combinations

questions.explanation: Why B: Splitting tools by exercise type makes invalid combinations structurally impossible. The correct parameters are enforced by the schema itself—no documentation, examples, or validation needed.

questions.title: Scheduling Agent: Slot No Longer Available
questions.text: get_available_slots then book_appointment. Another user books in between. How to redesign?

options.A: Modify book_appointment to return failure information including currently available alternative slots
options.B: Combine both tools into a single find_and_book_appointment that atomically checks and books
options.C: Add a hold_slot(provider_id, slot_time) tool that creates a 60-second temporary reservation, requiring the agent to call it between checking availability and booking
options.D: Keep both tools but add retry logic in the system prompt instructing the agent to call get_available_slots again if booking fails

questions.explanation: Why C: This directly solves the race condition by creating a temporary hold. The workflow becomes: get_available_slots → hold_slot → show options → book_appointment, with the held slot protected from concurrent booking.

questions.title: Document Extraction Confidence Scores
questions.text: (1) Agent proceeds with low-confidence extractions that are incorrect 23% of the time. (2) Agent requests unnecessary review for high-confidence extractions that are actually correct 31% of the time. How to restructure tool output?

options.A: Compute an aggregate extraction_quality score across all fields with a text summary
options.B: Return fields with raw confidence scores and add few-shot examples for interpretation
options.C: Return fields organized into verified and needs_verification objects based on confidence thresholds
options.D: Return fields with confidence scores, plus a requires_review boolean computed using your tested confidence thresholds, along with a review_reasons array explaining which fields triggered review

questions.explanation: Why D: The requires_review boolean (computed from calibrated, tested thresholds) directly solves both problems: it stops unnecessary reviews for correctly-extracted high-confidence fields, and it flags genuinely uncertain low-confidence fields. The review_reasons array helps human reviewers focus their attention.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Customer verification before refunds
questions.text: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your support agent skips get_customer and calls lookup_order using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. What change would most effectively address this?

options.A: Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks lookup_order and process_refund until get_customer returns a verified customer ID
options.B: Enhance the system prompt stating customer verification via get_customer is mandatory before any order operations
options.C: Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling get_customer first, even when customers volunteer order details
options.D: Implement a routing classifier that enables only the subset of tools appropriate for each request type

questions.explanation: When a specific tool sequence is required for critical business logic, programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees that prompt-based approaches cannot.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Tool selection with minimal descriptions
questions.text: The agent calls get_customer for order questions instead of lookup_order. Both tools have minimal descriptions and accept similar identifier formats. What is the most effective first step?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection patterns
options.B: Expand each tool's description to include input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries versus similar tools
options.C: Implement a routing layer that pre-selects the appropriate tool from keywords and identifier patterns
options.D: Consolidate both tools into a single lookup_entity tool that internally routes to the correct backend

questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection. Minimal descriptions are the root cause; expanding them is the highest-leverage first fix.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Escalation calibration
questions.text: The agent escalates straightforward cases while attempting complex policy exceptions autonomously. What is the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?

options.A: Add explicit escalation criteria to the system prompt with few-shot examples for when to escalate versus resolve autonomously
options.B: Have the agent self-report a confidence score and route to humans below a threshold
options.C: Deploy a separate classifier trained on historical tickets to predict escalation before the main agent runs
options.D: Implement sentiment analysis to escalate when negative sentiment exceeds a threshold

questions.explanation: Unclear decision boundaries are the root cause. Explicit criteria with few-shot examples directly address calibration before adding ML infrastructure.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Team slash command location
questions.text: You want a custom /review slash command available to every developer when they clone the repository. Where should you create this command file?

options.A: In the .claude/commands/ directory in the project repository
options.B: In ~/.claude/commands/ in each developer's home directory
options.C: In the CLAUDE.md file at the project root
options.D: In a .claude/config.json file with a commands array

questions.explanation: Project-scoped commands live in .claude/commands/ and are version-controlled for the whole team. ~/.claude/commands/ is personal only.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Monolith to microservices
questions.text: You will restructure a monolith across dozens of files with decisions about service boundaries. Which approach should you take?

options.A: Enter plan mode to explore the codebase and design an implementation approach before making changes
options.B: Start with direct execution and let implementation reveal natural service boundaries
options.C: Use direct execution with comprehensive upfront instructions for each service structure
options.D: Begin in direct execution and switch to plan mode only if unexpected complexity appears

questions.explanation: Plan mode is for large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, and architectural decisions—exactly this restructuring scenario.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Path-specific conventions
questions.text: Test files are spread throughout the codebase (Button.test.tsx next to Button.tsx). What is the most maintainable way to apply test conventions automatically when generating code?

options.A: Create rule files in .claude/rules/ with YAML frontmatter glob patterns (e.g. **/*.test.tsx)
options.B: Consolidate all conventions in root CLAUDE.md and rely on Claude to infer which section applies
options.C: Create skills in .claude/skills/ that include conventions in SKILL.md files
options.D: Place a separate CLAUDE.md in each subdirectory containing that area's conventions

questions.explanation: .claude/rules/ with globs applies conventions by file path regardless of directory—essential for tests spread across the repo.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Coordinator task decomposition
questions.text: A multi-agent research report on "AI in creative industries" covers only visual arts; logs show the coordinator assigned only digital art, graphic design, and photography subtasks. What is the most likely root cause?

options.A: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps
options.B: The coordinator's task decomposition is too narrow, omitting relevant domains of the topic
options.C: The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough
options.D: The document analysis agent filters out non-visual sources due to restrictive relevance criteria

questions.explanation: Subagents executed their assigned scope correctly; the coordinator's narrow decomposition omitted music, writing, and film.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Subagent timeout error propagation
questions.text: A web search subagent times out. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent coordinator recovery?

options.A: Return structured error context including failure type, attempted query, partial results, and potential alternatives
options.B: Implement automatic retry with exponential backoff in the subagent, returning generic "search unavailable" after exhaustion
options.C: Catch the timeout and return an empty result set marked as successful
options.D: Propagate the timeout to a top-level handler that terminates the entire workflow

questions.explanation: Structured error context lets the coordinator retry with a modified query, use partial results, or annotate coverage gaps.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Scoped verify_fact tool
questions.text: Simple fact-checks (85%) add 2-3 coordinator round trips when synthesis delegates all verification to web search. What reduces overhead while maintaining reliability?

options.A: Give the synthesis agent a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups; complex verifications still go through the coordinator to web search
options.B: Batch all verification needs at the end of synthesis for one coordinator→web search pass
options.C: Give the synthesis agent all web search tools to verify directly
options.D: Have web search proactively cache extra context anticipating synthesis verification needs

questions.explanation: Least privilege: scoped verify_fact for the common case; coordinator pattern preserved for complex investigation.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Claude Code in CI/CD
questions.text: claude "Analyze this pull request" hangs in CI waiting for interactive input. What is the correct non-interactive approach?

options.A: Add the -p flag: claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"
options.B: Set CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true before running the command
options.C: Redirect stdin from /dev/null
options.D: Add the --batch flag

questions.explanation: The -p (--print) flag runs Claude Code non-interactively: process prompt, write stdout, exit—required for pipelines.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Message Batches API tradeoff
questions.text: You have a blocking pre-merge check and an overnight technical debt report. Your manager proposes Message Batches API (50% savings) for both. How should you evaluate this?

options.A: Use batch processing for overnight reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks
options.B: Switch both to batch with status polling
options.C: Keep real-time for both to avoid batch ordering issues
options.D: Switch both to batch with timeout fallback to real-time

questions.explanation: Batch API has up to 24-hour processing with no latency SLA—unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks, ideal for overnight jobs.

questions.title: [Exam Guide] Multi-pass PR review
questions.text: A 14-file PR review in one pass yields inconsistent depth, missed bugs, and contradictory feedback. How should you restructure the review?

options.A: Split into per-file local analysis passes, then a separate integration pass for cross-file data flow
options.B: Require developers to split PRs to 3-4 files before review
options.C: Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window
options.D: Run three full-PR passes and only flag issues appearing in at least two runs

questions.explanation: Focused passes address attention dilution; a separate integration pass catches cross-file issues without suppressing real bugs via consensus.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Code Review – Severity Consistency
questions.text: Your automated code review system shows inconsistent severity ratings — similar issues like null pointer risks receive critical severity in some PRs but only medium in others. Developer trust is declining because teams cannot predict which findings require immediate attention. What is the most effective way to improve severity consistency?

options.A: Request that Claude include its reasoning for each severity assignment, then use that reasoning to manually calibrate ratings during review
options.B: Include explicit severity criteria in your prompt with concrete code examples for each severity level
options.C: Add a CLAUDE.md file that lists issue types and their default severities, instructing Claude to reference this mapping when assigning severity
options.D: Modify the prompt to ask Claude to rate severity relative to other issues in the same PR, so the most severe issue is always marked critical

questions.explanation: Without a rubric, Claude infers severity inconsistently. Explicit criteria with concrete code examples for each level gives Claude a grounded definition to match patterns to labels consistently.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Claude Code – Automated Pipeline
questions.text: Your pipeline script runs `claude "analyze this and report for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Claude Code is waiting for user input. What is the best approach to run Claude Code in an automated pipeline?

options.A: Redirect stdin from /dev/null
options.B: Add the --batch flag to the command
options.C: Add the -p flag: `claude -p "analyze this and report for security issues"`
options.D: Set an environment variable to enable non-interactive mode before running

questions.explanation: The -p (print/non-interactive) flag tells Claude Code to run once, output result to stdout, then exit without waiting for user input — the designed solution for CI/CD pipelines.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Code Review – False Positive Rate
questions.text: Analysis shows Security & correctness findings have 4% FP rate, style & naming have 45% FP rate, and documentation findings have 48% FP rate. Developers have started dismissing findings without review. What approach best restores developer trust while improving the system?

options.A: Keep all categories enabled while adding few-shot examples to improve each category accuracy over the coming weeks
options.B: Apply a uniform strictness reduction across all categories to bring the overall false positive rate to an acceptable level
options.C: Keep all categories but display a confidence score with each finding, letting developers decide which to investigate
options.D: Temporarily disable high false-positive categories (style, naming, documentation) and run only high-precision categories while improving precision

questions.explanation: High-FP categories destroy trust in accurate categories. D surgically keeps high-precision categories running while fixing noisy ones offline, restoring trust immediately.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Batch API – Document Routing
questions.text: Your extraction system processes standard monthly reports (archived after processing) and urgent exception reports (need trigger actions within 1 hour). Both use the same JSON schema. You want to minimize API costs while meeting latency requirements. How should you architect the processing pipeline?

options.A: Submit all documents to the real-time Messages API to ensure consistent processing latency across document types
options.B: Route standard reports to the Batch API for 50% cost savings, and route urgent exception reports to the real-time Messages API
options.C: Queue all documents and submit hourly batches, flagging urgent documents for expedited handling
options.D: Submit all documents to the Batch API with custom_ids for tracking, immediately process urgent documents when results arrive

questions.explanation: Match each document type to the right API: Batch API for cost savings on non-urgent reports, real-time Messages API for the 1-hour SLA on urgent exceptions.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Structured Output – JSON Reliability
questions.text: Your extraction pipeline occasionally receives responses that do not parse as valid JSON, causing downstream processing failures. What is the most reliable approach to ensure Claude returns valid, schema-compliant structured data?

options.A: Add explicit formatting instructions to the prompt with JSON examples, emphasizing that Claude must return only valid JSON with no surrounding text
options.B: Use regular expressions to locate and extract JSON from the response text, handling cases where Claude includes explanatory text
options.C: Define a tool with a JSON schema specifying the expected structure, using tool use to constrain Claude output to schema-compliant data
options.D: Implement a retry loop that catches JSON parse errors and re-prompts Claude with the error details, asking it to correct the validation issues

questions.explanation: Tool use with a JSON schema is API-level enforcement. Claude must fill structured parameters validated against the schema — an architectural constraint, not a prompt suggestion.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Human Review – Semantic Errors
questions.text: After deployment, 12% of extractions contain semantic errors that pass JSON schema validation (e.g., a duration like "30 minutes" stored in a numeric field expecting seconds). Human reviewers can only check 20% of extractions. Which approach most effectively allocates reviewer attention?

options.A: Prioritize review of all extractions where required fields are empty or explicitly marked as not found
options.B: Randomly sample 20% of extractions for review, using corrections to track accuracy and identify error patterns
options.C: Have the model output field-level confidence scores, then calibrate review thresholds using a labeled validation set
options.D: Review all extractions from documents with formatting anomalies such as unusual layouts or mixed content types

questions.explanation: Field-level confidence scores with calibrated thresholds on a labeled validation set directs reviewers to extractions with the highest probability of semantic errors — risk-based prioritization.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Tool Use – Forcing Specific Tools
questions.text: Your pipeline uses `extract_metadata` with a JSON schema. You also defined `citation_formatter` and `reference_list` tools. Claude sometimes calls `citation_formatter` first, which fails because structured metadata extraction must happen first. What is the most effective way to ensure `extract_metadata` is called first?

options.A: Set `tool_choice` to "any" so Claude must use a tool, combined with system prompt instructions prioritizing `extract_metadata`
options.B: Set `tool_choice` to {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} and process subsequent calls in the pipeline
options.C: Set `tool_choice` to "auto" and reorder the tool definitions so `extract_metadata` appears first
options.D: Set `tool_choice` to "auto" so `extract_metadata` appears first in the tool array, ensuring Claude prioritizes it

questions.explanation: tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} forces Claude to call that specific tool. "any" and "auto" only suggest or require some tool — they do not guarantee which one.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Semantic Validation – Retry Logic
questions.text: Your invoice extraction uses tool use with strict JSON schemas. JSON syntax errors never occur, but 12% of extractions fail semantic validation (line items do not sum to total, vendor IDs wrong format). These currently route to manual review. What is the most effective approach to reduce manual review while maintaining accuracy?

options.A: Implement post-processing logic that automatically corrects common errors, such as recalculating total from line items when sums do not match
options.B: When validation fails, make a follow-up request with the document, extraction, and validation errors for model correction
options.C: Retry the extraction up to 3 times when validation fails, accepting the first result that passes validation
options.D: Add stricter schema constraints with detailed field descriptions to prevent the model from generating invalid values

questions.explanation: Targeted correction with validation error context lets Claude see the original document, what it extracted, and exactly what failed — enabling precise fixes with full context.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Retry Logic – When It Fails
questions.text: Your extraction system implements automatic retries when validation fails. Each retry appends the specific validation error to the prompt and resolves most failures within 2-3 attempts. For which failure pattern would additional retries be LEAST effective?

options.A: The model extracts citation counts as locale-formatted strings ("1,234") when the schema requires integers
options.B: The model extracts keywords as a nested object organized by category when the schema requires a flat array of strings
options.C: The model extracts "et al." for co-authors when the full author list exists only in an external document not in the input
options.D: The model extracts dates as ISO 8601 datetime strings ("2023-03-15T00:00:00") when the schema requires only the date portion

questions.explanation: Retries fix how Claude outputs data, not what data is available. The full co-author list does not exist in the input — no amount of retrying can produce information that is not there.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Conflicting Data Sources
questions.text: Specifications sometimes appear inconsistently within source documents. A summary section might state "Battery: 4000mAh" while the detailed specs table shows "Battery: 4200mAh." Your schema uses a single `battery_capacity_mah` field. The detailed specs table is more accurate 90% of the time. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Implement schema validation that rejects results containing conflicting values, requiring manual review for affected documents
options.B: Add a `conflict_detected` boolean field that flags inconsistencies, triggering manual review for affected documents
options.C: Include extraction instructions specifying to prefer values from the detailed specs table when multiple values exist
options.D: Change to an array field that captures all values found with their source locations, letting downstream systems apply preference rules

questions.explanation: You already know the detailed specs table is more reliable 90% of the time. Apply that knowledge directly at extraction time rather than routing to manual review or over-engineering with arrays.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Context Window – Tool Definition Tokens
questions.text: Your extraction system uses tool use with a JSON schema containing 12 fields totaling approximately 2,100 tokens. For documents under 150K tokens, accuracy is 98%. For documents between 135–190K tokens, accuracy drops to 71%, with information from the first half rarely extracted accurately. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: Very long documents exceed the model effective attention span regardless of context limits
options.B: Schemas exceeding 8-10 fields increase decision complexity during parameter generation, independent of document length
options.C: Tool definitions consume input context tokens. Combined with system prompts and document content, the total approaches or exceeds the context window, causing truncation of earlier document content
options.D: The model distributes attention proportionally across input context length, causing fields mentioned early to receive less relative attention

questions.explanation: Document (150–190K) + tool definition (~2,100) + system prompt approaches the 200K context window. When exceeded, earlier content gets truncated — explaining why the first half is not extracted accurately.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Long Document Strategy
questions.text: Evaluation shows 94% extraction accuracy on short meeting transcripts (<30 minutes) but only 64% on longer transcripts (>60 minutes) where key decisions are scattered throughout. Both lengths fit within the model context window. What pattern most effectively improves accuracy on longer documents?

options.A: Add few-shot examples demonstrating correct extraction from lengthy meetings with scattered information
options.B: Upgrade to a more capable model tier for the extraction task
options.C: Split lengthy transcripts into chunks, extract from each chunk separately, then merge and deduplicate the results
options.D: Add a pre-extraction step where the model summarizes key discussions and conclusions, then perform extraction on that summary

questions.explanation: This is a signal-to-noise problem, not truncation. A 2-step pipeline — summarize first to concentrate signal, then extract from the clean summary — addresses scattered information in long transcripts.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Output Normalization
questions.text: Your extraction pipeline processes restaurant menus and must output structured JSON with fields for item names, descriptions, prices, and dietary info. Source menus are inconsistent: prices appear as "$12" vs "12.00", dietary info as icons vs text. What is the most reliable approach for consistent output?

options.A: Use separate extraction calls for each field to ensure consistent handling of each type
options.B: Extract data as-is and normalize formats in post-processing code after Claude returns
options.C: Define a strict output schema AND include format normalization rules in your prompt
options.D: Request multiple extraction attempts per document and select the most common format

questions.explanation: Two-layer enforcement: strict schema defines structure and types; normalization rules in the prompt tell Claude how to convert (e.g., extract prices as numeric values without currency symbols).

questions.title: [Real Exam] Few-Shot Examples – Nuanced Behavior
questions.text: Your schema includes a `skills: string[]` field. Production monitoring reveals: (1) compound phrases like "Python and SQL" are occasionally split, (2) implied but unstated skills occasionally appear, (3) similar documents produce wildly different array lengths (2–16 entries). Your prompt currently says "list all skills mentioned." What is the most effective improvement?

options.A: Add post-extraction normalization that maps skills to a canonical taxonomy and deduplicates similar entries
options.B: Add constraints: "Extract 10–20 skills maximum, one skill per entry, only explicitly named skills"
options.C: Enrich the schema to `{skill: string, confidence: float, source: string}` to capture extraction metadata
options.D: Add few-shot examples demonstrating compound phrase handling, explicit mention criteria, and appropriate entry volumes

questions.explanation: All three problems are nuanced behavioral issues. Few-shot examples show Claude exactly how to handle compound phrases, implied skills, and appropriate entry counts — addressing all three simultaneously.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Batch Processing Strategy
questions.text: Your team is extracting structured data from 50,000 legacy legal contracts under a two-week deadline. Initial testing with 500 sample documents achieves 82% success on the first attempt. Which batch processing strategy is most cost-efficient while still meeting the deadline?

options.A: Use the real-time API for all 50,000 documents since the batch API 24-hour processing window creates unacceptable deadline risk
options.B: Split documents into 10 sequential batches of 5,000 each, analyzing results and refining prompts between batches
options.C: Submit all 50,000 documents via batch API, then submit failed extractions via real-time API to identify failure patterns
options.D: Process ~2,000 sample documents via real-time API to identify failure patterns and refine prompts, then batch process all 50,000 with the corrected prompt

questions.explanation: Learn first, scale second: real-time on 2,000 docs surfaces failure patterns, refine prompts, then submit all 50,000 via Batch API for 50% cost savings within the two-week deadline.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Multi-Domain Prompts
questions.text: Your automated reviewer uses a single prompt covering security issues, API design, and business logic correctness. Business logic edge cases achieve only 34% recall. When you add few-shot examples for business logic, recall improves to 47% but API design recall drops. How do you address this trade-off?

options.A: Split the review into separate focused prompts — one for security and API design, another for business logic — each with dedicated examples
options.B: Provide the full repository as context instead of just the changed files to give the model deeper visibility
options.C: Upgrade to a more capable model tier, since its stronger reasoning will handle both concern types in a single prompt
options.D: Replace the few-shot examples with a detailed checklist of specific logic edge cases to verify

questions.explanation: One prompt covering multiple review types forces Claude to split focus. Splitting into domain-focused prompts with dedicated examples eliminates the competing attention problem.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Branch-Level Coverage
questions.text: Your code review prompts include both implementation changes and the corresponding test file. The LLM correctly flags functions with no tests but fails to identify when conditional branches or error handling paths within tested functions lack coverage. What is the most effective way to improve detection without overcomplicating the pipeline?

options.A: Add explicit instructions directing the model to enumerate each conditional branch and exception path, then verify each has a corresponding test case
options.B: Include few-shot examples showing code with an uncovered branch paired with the review comment identifying the specific missing test case
options.C: Restructure the prompt to use two separate LLM calls: first extract all conditional branches, then identify missing coverage
options.D: Implement a multi-pass pipeline where separate LLM calls first extract branches, then identify missing coverage

questions.explanation: Claude understands missing tests at the function level but not branch-level gaps. Few-shot examples directly teach this pattern recognition in a single pass without over-complicating the pipeline.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Cross-File Bug Detection
questions.text: Your pipeline reviews every PR using a single API call with a static prompt containing the diff and full text of each changed file. Reviews consistently miss bugs involving cross-file interactions. Cross-file bugs account for 35% of production incidents. What is the most cost-effective change?

options.A: Redesign the review as a turn-limited agentic task where the model can read files and search the codebase via tools
