options.B: Run parallel review passes per changed file with direct dependents included in each pass, then aggregate findings
options.C: Use static analysis to build a dependency graph of changed code, then expand the prompt to include all files within two hops of the changed files
options.D: Add chain-of-thought instructions asking the model to list all external references in the diff, then reason about cross-file impact

questions.explanation: Static analysis deterministically identifies dependent files. Include files within 2 hops in one API call — most cost-effective because it adds only relevant context without multiple tool calls or parallel passes.

questions.title: [Real Exam] CLAUDE.md – Suppressing False Positives
questions.text: The automated review consistently flags patterns your team uses intentionally: force-unwrapping optionals in test files, using large coordinate structs, importing internally-maintained deprecated modules. Developers are dismissing roughly 30% of all findings. What prevents the model from generating these findings by supplying project conventions as persistent context?

options.A: Build post-processing keyword filters that suppress findings containing terms like "force unwrap" or "deprecated import"
options.B: Document the team accepted patterns and intentional conventions in the project CLAUDE.md file
options.C: Have developers add inline suppress-comments at flagged lines in the diff
options.D: Configure the review to analyze only the changed lines in the diff without surrounding file context

questions.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the designed mechanism for project-level persistent context. Claude reads team conventions before every review and stops generating false positives for intentional patterns.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Claude Code – Resource Caps
questions.text: During testing, reviews on large PRs (50+ changed files) sometimes take over 30 minutes and cost $12 per run. Your team needs each invocation to abort once it reaches a fixed iteration count AND a hard dollar amount — enforced by Claude Code itself, not the surrounding job runner. Which configuration change directly enforces both caps?

options.A: Add `--max-turns 10 --max-budget-usd 2.00` to the `claude` invocation
options.B: Switch the --model flag to a smaller, cheaper model so each iteration uses fewer tokens
options.C: Set `timeout-minutes: 5` on the GitHub Actions job step and monitor per-run costs via Anthropic Console
options.D: Set `--permission-mode dontAsk` to auto-deny any tool permission requests not in the explicitly allowed set

questions.explanation: --max-turns caps agentic iterations and --max-budget-usd caps dollar amount. Both flags are built into Claude Code CLI and enforced by Claude Code itself.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Output Token Limit
questions.text: Your automated review uses tool_use with a `report_findings` tool returning a JSON array. On a large PR touching 30+ files, the response hits the `max_tokens` limit and is truncated mid-JSON, causing the downstream parser to crash. What is the most effective way to handle this?

options.A: Switch from tool_use to prompting Claude to return findings as a markdown list, reporting only critical and high severity findings
options.B: Add retry logic that detects truncated JSON and re-sends the request with instructions to report only critical/high severity findings
options.C: Split the review into multiple API calls that each analyze a subset of the changed files, then merge the resulting findings arrays
options.D: Increase `max_tokens` to the model maximum and instruct Claude to keep finding descriptions under 50 words each

questions.explanation: Too much output from a single call causes truncation. Split input into batches (5-10 files per call), merge findings arrays — scales as PR sizes grow without losing severity coverage.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Batch API Feasibility
questions.text: Your CI pipeline performs security-focused code reviews on approximately 50 PRs daily, currently costing $150/day. You are evaluating the Message Batches API for its 50% cost reduction. What factor most determines whether batch processing is actionable for this use case?

options.A: Whether review feedback arriving up to 24 hours after PR creation remains actionable for your workflow
options.B: Whether reducing per-review latency from 30-60 seconds to near-instant matters for your workflow
options.C: Whether your result processing can handle reviews arriving in a different order than submitted
options.D: Whether you can structure each review as a single request without multi-turn refinement

questions.explanation: The Batch API trade-off is 50% cheaper but results take up to 24 hours. Whether developers can tolerate that delay is the binary, workflow-specific determining factor.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Claude Code – Startup Time
questions.text: Your automated review CI jobs take 18 seconds to initialize before Claude begins analyzing code. Profiling reveals the delay comes from auto-discovery of CLAUDE.md files throughout your monorepo. You need to cut startup time while ensuring reviews still enforce team coding standards in your top-level CLAUDE.md. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Run with `--bare` mode and pass `--append-system-prompt-file /CLAUDE.md` to explicitly load your project standards while skipping all auto-discovery
options.B: Keep the default initialization and add `--exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections` to reduce prompt size
options.C: Replace the default prompt entirely using `--system-prompt-file /CLAUDE.md`
options.D: Run with `--bare` mode and specify all review criteria directly in the `-p` prompt argument for each CI invocation

questions.explanation: --bare skips auto-discovery (eliminates the 18-second monorepo scan). --append-system-prompt-file explicitly loads the top-level CLAUDE.md, preserving team standards.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Precision vs Recall
questions.text: After deploying automated code review, you notice high precision but low recall — real bugs are slipping through. The prompt instructs Claude to "only report high-confidence issues" and "err on the side of not commenting." What is the most effective way to substantially improve bug detection while keeping false positives manageable?

options.A: Remove the conservative filtering instructions and prompt Claude to report all potential issues, then apply a programmatic filter to remove false positives
options.B: Expand the context window by including related test files, git history, and the module dependency graph
options.C: Split the review into a finding stage where Claude flags every potential issue with confidence and severity scores, then apply risk-scoring to maintain current precision levels
options.D: Add detailed few-shot examples demonstrating the bug categories Claude should flag with the instruction to maintain current precision levels

questions.explanation: Two-stage pipeline decouples recall from precision: Stage 1 detects all potential issues with scores (high recall), Stage 2 filters by risk score to surface only actionable findings (controlled precision).

questions.title: [Real Exam] Self-Review Bias
questions.text: A developer uses Claude Code to refactor a function during their development session. Before committing, they ask the same Claude session to review the code. A separate automated CI review catches several bugs that the same-session review missed. What best explains this discrepancy?

options.A: The CI environment has access to the full codebase context while the local session only has the current file
options.B: Claude retains context about its prior reasoning in the session, making it less likely to question its own decisions
options.C: The CI review uses a more specific prompt tailored for catching bugs, while the developer request was more general
options.D: The extended session length caused the context window to fill with conversation history during the coding session

questions.explanation: Anchoring bias: Claude refactored the code and has reasoning in context for why it made each decision. When asked to review the same code, it is less likely to critically challenge its own prior decisions.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Batch API – Cost Reduction
questions.text: Your pipeline includes a release notes generation step that classifies and summarizes approximately 200 commits at the end of each weekly release cycle. Each commit requires a separate API call using a Sonnet-tier model. Results are not needed until the following morning (8+ hours acceptable wait). You want to reduce per-token API cost while keeping the same model and prompts. Which approach best reduces cost?

options.A: Issue the 200 Messages API requests in parallel using concurrent connections, since concurrency lowers the per-token price
options.B: Concatenate all 200 commit messages into a single Messages API request
options.C: Submit the 200 requests to the Message Batches API with unique custom_ids, collect results next morning
options.D: Switch the summarization calls from Sonnet-tier to Haiku-tier model

questions.explanation: Batch API provides same model, same prompts, 50% cost savings, results within 24 hours. Concurrency does not lower per-token price; changing model or prompts violates the constraints.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Prompt Filtering – Finding Suppression
questions.text: Your automated code review is missing genuine bugs. Investigation reveals your review prompt includes: "Only flag critical issues that would cause immediate production failures. Ignore minor concerns and anything you are uncertain about." Which prompt change best resolves missed bugs without breaking structured, tagged output for downstream filtering?

options.A: Enable extended thinking and instruct the model to reason step-by-step about each code change before producing its review
options.B: Remove all severity-related instructions from the prompt and let the model use its default judgment
options.C: Instruct the model to report ALL findings with a confidence level and severity tag, deferring filtering to downstream
options.D: Add a second review pass that re-reads the diff using the same prompt, looking for anything the first pass may have missed

questions.explanation: The problem is filtering inside the prompt. C fixes this by having Claude report everything with confidence/severity metadata while downstream filtering decides what to surface.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Claude Code – Cross-File Tool Access
questions.text: Your pipeline runs: `claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions --system-prompt "$PROMPT" < diff.txt`. Reviews complete but Claude only comments on the piped diff — it never reads surrounding files. Which change will cause Claude to read related files?

options.A: Stop piping the diff via stdin and instead embed the diff contents inside the prompt string
options.B: Replace `--system-prompt` with `--append-system-prompt` and add file navigation tools
options.C: Remove `--system-prompt` and add `--allowedTools` with file-reading tools
options.D: Keep `--system-prompt` AND add `--allowedTools` to include file-reading/code navigation tools

questions.explanation: You need both: system prompt for review instructions AND allowedTools for file-reading capability. --allowedTools controls what Claude can access; the system prompt contains the instructions.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Feedback Loop – Dismissed Findings
questions.text: You built an LLM-powered code review tool that outputs structured findings with `category` and `description`. Developers can click dismiss on findings they consider unhelpful — currently 30% are dismissed. What change to your output structure would best help you understand what your system is getting wrong?

options.A: Add a `detected_pattern` field recording the code construct (e.g., "single-letter loop variable") that triggered each finding
options.B: Expand the `description` field to include more detailed explanations of why each issue matters and how to fix it
options.C: Remove the `issue_category` field and track dismissals at the individual finding level
options.D: Add a `model_confidence` field (0.0–1.0) and filter out findings below a threshold before final output

questions.explanation: The `detected_pattern` field creates a feedback loop: dismissed finding → look at the pattern → identify over-flagging → fix the prompt/rules.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Context-Specific False Positives
questions.text: After deploying automated code review, approximately 35% of flagged findings are false positives: team conventions flagged as issues, security warnings for patterns safe in your deployment context, and performance suggestions that hurt your specific use case. Which approach best enables the model to generalise to novel code patterns it has not seen before?

options.A: Implement post-processing that uses keyword matching to filter out findings containing terms like "conventions" or "context-dependent"
options.B: Include few-shot examples in your prompt showing annotated code snippets that should NOT be flagged, explaining WHY they are acceptable
options.C: Create a comprehensive written specification of all patterns that should not be flagged
options.D: Add instructions to your system prompt to "be conservative" and "only flag definite issues"

questions.explanation: Few-shot examples teach the reasoning principle behind why something is acceptable. When Claude learns the principle from examples, it can apply that reasoning to new patterns it has never seen.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Agentic Loop – Stop Reason
questions.text: A customer contacts the agent about a warranty claim on a power drill. Resolving this requires multiple sequential tool calls: `get_customer`, `get_product`, and then either `process_refund` or `unable_to_honor`. What is the primary mechanism your application uses to determine whether to continue the loop or stop?

options.A: You check the `stop_reason` field in each API response — the loop continues while it equals "tool_use" and stops when it changes to "end_turn" or another terminal value
options.B: You manually set the `tool_choice` parameter to "none" after the final expected tool call to force Claude to stop requesting tools
options.C: You track the number of tool calls made and exit the loop once a predefined maximum is reached
options.D: You check whether Claude response contains a text content block — if text is present, the agent has produced its final answer

questions.explanation: stop_reason is the official API mechanism: stop_reason = "tool_use" → execute tool and continue; stop_reason = "end_turn" → stop loop and return final response.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Compliance – Hard Enforcement
questions.text: Compliance requires that refunds exceeding $500 must automatically escalate to a human agent — this rule cannot be left to model discretion. However, logs show the agent occasionally processes high-value refunds directly (5% failure rate). How should you achieve guaranteed human escalation?

options.A: Implement a hook to intercept tool calls: when the refund amount exceeds $500, block it and initiate human escalation programmatically
options.B: Add few-shot examples to the prompt showing correct escalation behavior at various refund amounts
options.C: Modify the refund tool to return an error with message "Amount exceeds policy limit — please transfer to human operator"
options.D: Strengthen the system prompt with emphatic language: "CRITICAL POLICY: All refunds over $500 MUST trigger human escalation"

questions.explanation: When a rule cannot be left to model discretion, enforce it in application code. A programmatic hook blocks the tool call at the infrastructure level — 100% reliable regardless of model behavior.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Structured Error Metadata
questions.text: Production logs reveal inconsistent error handling: when `lookup_order` fails, the agent sometimes retries (wrong for permanent errors), sometimes asks users for clarification (wrong for transient errors). Current error response: `{"isError": true, "content": [{"text": "Transaction Failed"}]}`. What change most improves error handling?

options.A: Enhance error responses with structured metadata: include `errorCategory` field (transient/validation/permission) so the agent knows how to respond
options.B: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff in your MCP server for all errors, returning to the agent only after all retries are exhausted
options.C: Create an `analyze_error` MCP tool the agent calls after any failure to determine the error category and appropriate action
options.D: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating how to interpret error message patterns and select appropriate responses

questions.explanation: The MCP server knows the error type — it should communicate that via structured metadata so the agent can retry transient errors, explain validation issues, or handle permission errors appropriately.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Escalation – Explicit Customer Request
questions.text: A customer sends: "This is frustrating. I have explained my issue twice and nothing is being resolved. I want to talk to a real person NOW." The agent has all customer account information and conversation history. The agent cannot resolve the specific issue anyway. What should the agent do?

options.A: Acknowledge the frustration and ask one targeted question to understand the specific issue before escalating
options.B: Immediately call `escalate_to_human` with the conversation history
options.C: First call `get_customer` and `lookup_order` to gather account context, then escalate to a human agent
options.D: Briefly explain what the agent can help with and offer to resolve the issue quickly

questions.explanation: Customer explicitly demanded a human NOW, agent cannot resolve the issue, and agent already has all context. Escalate immediately with conversation history so the human agent has full context.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Actionable Error Messages
questions.text: Your agent uses `lookup_order` which catches all exceptions and returns a tool_result with `is_error: true` and message "That operation failed." The agent retries identically or escalates immediately. Which change gives Claude the information it needs to select the correct recovery action?

options.A: Return error-type-specific messages with `is_error: true`, e.g., "Order not found (id: {id}): verify the order ID" and "Connection timeout: try lookup_order again, or use lookup_order_archive"
options.B: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff inside each tool implementation, returning normal results to the agent
options.C: Add an error classification step in the agentic loop that intercepts tool errors before Claude sees them and routes appropriately
options.D: Remove `is_error: true` and return error details as normal tool content

questions.explanation: Error messages are instructions for Claude. Specific messages enable correct decisions: "Order not found: verify order ID" → do not retry same ID; "Connection timeout: try again" → retry is valid.

questions.title: [Real Exam] MCP Tool Error Handling
questions.text: When implementing your `lookup_order` MCP tool, the backend sometimes returns errors (e.g., "Order not found" or temporary database failures). What is the correct way to handle these errors back to the agent?

options.A: Log the error server-side and return an empty result to avoid confusing the model
options.B: Throw an exception from the tool handler so the agent framework can catch and log it
options.C: Return the error message in the tool result content with the `is_error` flag set to true
options.D: Return a success response with a "status" field indicating the error type

questions.explanation: The official Anthropic API mechanism: is_error: true + descriptive message in content. Claude knows the tool failed, knows why, and can reason about recovery.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Context Management – Verbose Tool Output
questions.text: Your agent has called `lookup_order` multiple times while investigating a customer return requests. Each response includes 40+ fields, making tool outputs represent the majority of the conversation context. The customer mentions two more orders they want to discuss. What is the most efficient way to avoid consuming additional context for future lookups?

options.A: Move all tool responses to a vector database with semantic indexing, retrieving relevant portions as the conversation continues
options.B: Proceed with additional lookups without modifying the existing tool output content
options.C: Have the model generate a natural language summary of each order key details to replace the existing tool output context
options.D: Extract only return-relevant fields (items, purchase date, return window, return status) and replace the full tool output with just those fields

questions.explanation: For return requests, only 5-6 fields matter out of 40+. Filter tool output to return-relevant fields at extraction time — context consumption drops dramatically per lookup.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Mid-Process Tool Failure
questions.text: During a billing dispute resolution, your agent successfully retrieves customer info via `get_customer` and order details via `lookup_order`, but when attempting `process_refund`, it returns a timeout error. The agent has enough information to explain the charges and verify refund eligibility but cannot actually process the refund. What response best balances first-contact resolution with appropriate error handling?

options.A: Explain the billing, confirm refund eligibility, acknowledge the system issue preventing immediate processing, and offer escalation or a callback
options.B: Escalate immediately to a human agent since the refund action cannot be completed
options.C: Confirm the refund will be processed and close the conversation, since the agent has all necessary information
options.D: Implement automatic retries with exponential backoff for `process_refund`, keeping the conversation open while processing

questions.explanation: Do what you can, be honest about what you cannot, provide a path forward. Customer gets billing explanation and eligibility confirmation with a clear next step.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Escalation Triggers
questions.text: You are implementing escalation logic for when the agent should call `escalate_to_human`. Which approach most reliably identifies cases that genuinely require human intervention?

options.A: Instruct the agent to escalate when: the customer requests a human, the issue requires policy exceptions, or the agent cannot make progress toward resolution
options.B: Implement sentiment analysis that monitors for frustration indicators (negative language, exclamation marks) and triggers escalation when reaching a threshold
options.C: Build a rules engine that maps specific issue types, customer segments, and product categories to escalation decisions
options.D: Configure the agent to escalate after three consecutive tool calls that fail to resolve the customer issue

questions.explanation: Semantic escalation triggers describe actual situations where human intervention adds value: customer wants a human, policy exception needed, or no progress possible.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Returning Customer – Stale Data
questions.text: A customer returns 4 hours after their initial session about the same billing dispute. The previous 32-turn session contains tool results from 4 hours ago. When resuming, the agent references OLD data even after fresh tool calls return different results. What approach most reliably handles returning customers?

options.A: Resume with full history and add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to always prefer the most recent tool results
options.B: Start a new session, inject a structured summary of the previous interaction (issue type, actions taken, resolution status) — forces all data lookups to be fresh
options.C: Resume with full history and configure the agent to automatically re-call all previously used tools at session start
options.D: Resume with full history but filter out previous tool result messages, keeping only the most recent tool calls

questions.explanation: New session eliminates stale tool results. Structured summary preserves customer continuity. All tool calls in the new session are 100% fresh with no anchoring to old data.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Mid-Process Escalation
questions.text: Your agent is handling a billing dispute. After calling `get_customer` and `lookup_order`, it identifies the dispute involves a promotional pricing error requiring manager approval — beyond the agent authorization level. How should the workflow handle this mid-process escalation?

options.A: Compile a structured handoff (customer details + order info + identified issue) then call `escalate_to_human` with a reference ID
options.B: Persist the complete conversation and tool response history to a database, then call `escalate_to_human`
options.C: Attempt `process_refund` anyway, escalating only if the refund fails
options.D: Call `escalate_to_human` passing only the customer original message

questions.explanation: Preserve all gathered intelligence in a structured handoff. Human agent receives complete context: customer details, order info, root cause, and reference ID — no customer re-explanation needed.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Multi-Turn Memory
questions.text: The agent verifies customer identity through a multi-step process before resetting passwords. During testing, after the customer answers security questions, the agent asks them to provide their name again, as if the earlier exchange never happened. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The conversation history is not being passed in subsequent API requests
options.B: The prompt lacks instructions telling Claude to remember information across multiple exchanges
options.C: The verification tool is clearing the agent internal state after each successful validation step
options.D: Claude memory retention is limited to two conversation turns by default, requiring explicit configuration to extend

questions.explanation: Claude has zero persistent memory between API calls. If the application fails to include prior messages in the API request, Claude starts fresh every call — the most common multi-turn implementation bug.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Escalation Content – What to Pass
questions.text: After investigating a billing dispute over 25+ turns, you identified that duplicate charges occurred due to a payment gateway timeout. The refund exceeds your $500 authorization limit. You must call `escalate_to_human`. The human agent will not have access to your conversation history. What content should you pass?

options.A: The complete conversation transcript with all tool results
options.B: Your diagnosis and the refund amount only
options.C: A structured summary: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action
options.D: The customer original complaint verbatim plus tool result excerpts showing duplicate transactions

questions.explanation: Human agent needs exactly what they need: customer ID, root cause, refund amount, and recommended action — clean, structured, immediately actionable.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Agentic Decision Making
questions.text: When the agent calls `lookup_order` and receives order details showing the item was purchased 45 days ago, how does the agentic loop determine whether to call `escalate_to_human` next?

options.A: The agent executes the remaining steps in a tool sequence planned at the start of the request
options.B: The order details are added to the conversation and the model reasons about which action to take next
options.C: The agent follows a pre-configured decision tree mapping order attributes to specific tool calls
options.D: The orchestration layer automatically routes to the next tool based on the order status field

questions.explanation: Core agentic loop mechanism: tool_result appended to conversation → new API call → Claude reasons about next tool or final response based on accumulated context.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Guaranteeing Outcomes
questions.text: Production logs show that when the agent handles complex billing disputes requiring 6+ tool calls, it sometimes exhausts its `max_turns` limit without resolving or escalating. The goal is to guarantee every interaction ends with either `process_refund` OR `escalate_to_human`. Which approach achieves this guarantee?

options.A: Split the workflow into two sequential agent invocations with separate turn budgets
options.B: Implement a pre-tool-use hook that terminates the loop and escalates when approaching the maximum call count
options.C: Add system prompt instructions telling the agent to call `escalate_to_human` if the loop ends without resolution
options.D: Add orchestration layer code that checks the agent outcome after each loop terminates — if neither `process_refund` nor `escalate_to_human` was called, call `escalate_to_human` with accumulated context

questions.explanation: Post-loop safety net in application code: after loop completes for any reason, if neither target tool was called, orchestration forces escalation. Works for all termination causes including max_turns.

questions.title: [Real Exam] CLAUDE.md Debugging
questions.text: You documented API error handling conventions in a CLAUDE.md file at your project root. Claude Code sometimes follows these conventions and sometimes uses generic try/catch blocks. The inconsistency appears across different teams. What is the most efficient first diagnostic step?

options.A: Run `/memory` to check which memory files are loaded and verify your CLAUDE.md is included
options.B: Add more detailed code examples to your CLAUDE.md with YAML frontmatter showing the exact error handling patterns
options.C: Search for conflicting instructions in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` that might override your project conventions
options.D: Create path-specific rules in `.claude/rules/` with YAML frontmatter specifying the error handling conventions

questions.explanation: /memory shows which context files are loaded. This 5-second check answers the fundamental question: is my CLAUDE.md even in Claude context? Always diagnose before fixing.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Plan Mode vs Direct Execution
questions.text: A critical bug is affecting production users. Error logs show exceptions in the OrderProcessing module with a clear stack trace pointing to a specific function. No human has worked on this codebase before. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Use direct execution to examine the stack trace, read the relevant code, and implement a fix once you identify the root cause
options.B: Start with direct execution to gather initial information, then switch to plan mode to design a comprehensive solution before implementing
options.C: Use plan mode to analyze the error in context of the module architecture and dependencies before attempting any fixes
options.D: Enter plan mode to explore the module architecture and enumerate potential root causes before attempting any fixes

questions.explanation: Clear stack trace + specific function identified + production is down = direct execution. Plan mode is for ambiguous, wide-scope problems — planning overhead is waste when the stack trace hands you the answer.

questions.title: [Real Exam] MCP Server Scopes
questions.text: Your team is configuring MCP servers in Claude Code. You want to add a shared venue lookup server that all team members should have access to, and a personal music playlist server that only you are testing. Which configuration approach correctly applies MCP server scopes?

options.A: Add both servers to your local `~/.claude.json`
options.B: Add venue server to `.mcp.json` and playlist server to `~/.claude.json`
options.C: Add both servers to the project-level `.mcp.json` file
options.D: Add venue server to `~/.claude.json` and playlist server to `.mcp.json`

questions.explanation: .mcp.json (project-level) is committed to repo for all team members. ~/.claude.json (user-level) is personal machine only. Venue = shared, playlist = personal.

questions.title: [Real Exam] PostToolUse Hook – Formatting
questions.text: Your team CLAUDE.md includes: "Use 4-space indentation and always run Prettier formatting." Code reviews reveal roughly 15% of Claude-generated code violates this. Adding emphatic instructions reduces violations to 15% but does not eliminate them. What is the most effective way to ensure all generated code is consistently formatted?

options.A: Split the formatting rules into path-scoped `.claude/rules/` files that load when Claude works on matching file types
options.B: Configure a PostToolUse hook with an Edit/Write matcher that automatically runs Prettier on each file Claude modifies
options.C: Add a Stop hook with a prompt-based check that evaluates whether generated code follows formatting standards
options.D: Extract the formatting rules into a dedicated skill with more detailed examples of correct formatting

questions.explanation: Prompt engineering ceiling has been hit at 15% violations. PostToolUse hook triggers automatically after every Edit/Write — Prettier runs regardless of what Claude generated. 100% of file writes get formatted.

questions.title: [Real Exam] MCP Prompts – Slash Commands
questions.text: Your team has connected a custom MCP server that provides DevOps workflow templates. The server exposes several MCP prompts along with tools. How do these MCP prompts become accessible within Claude Code?

options.A: They are added to Claude Code tool registry alongside the server tools, invoked automatically by the model when relevant
options.B: They are automatically prepended to every conversation as additional system-level context
options.C: They are surfaced as @-mentionable resources alongside files, fetched and attached to your message when referenced
options.D: They appear as slash commands (e.g., `/mcp__servername__deploy_checklist`) that you can invoke with arguments

questions.explanation: MCP primitives have different access patterns: Tools → automatic calls, Resources → @-mentionable, Prompts → slash commands (/mcp__servername__promptname).

questions.title: [Real Exam] Skills – Version Controlled
questions.text: Your team frequently migrates React components to Vue. You want to register this as `/migrate-component`, and the workflow should stay in sync as the team iterates on it. Where should you place the skill file?

options.A: In the project `.claude/settings.json` using a `skillOverrides` entry on each developer machine
options.B: In `~/.claude/skills/migrate-component/SKILL.md` at the project root, committed to version control
options.C: In `.claude/skills/migrate-component/SKILL.md` at the project root, committed to version control
options.D: As a detailed instruction block in the project root CLAUDE.md file

questions.explanation: .claude/skills/ (project-level) committed to version control = shared with all team members and stays in sync. ~/.claude/skills/ is personal/local only.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Bug Fixing – Test Cases
questions.text: You asked Claude to write a data migration script, but the initial output does not correctly handle records with null values in required fields. What is the most efficient path to a working solution?

options.A: Add "think harder about edge cases" to your prompt and request a complete rewrite of the migration logic
options.B: Manually edit the generated code to fix the null handling, then continue working with Claude on other parts
options.C: Describe the null value problem in detail and ask Claude to regenerate the entire script with improved edge case handling
options.D: Provide a test case with example input containing null values and the expected output, then ask Claude to fix it

questions.explanation: Concrete test case (input + expected output) gives Claude the most precise specification. Targeted fix for the exact failing case — no unnecessary full regeneration.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Configuration Mechanism Mapping
questions.text: Your team has three requirements: (1) Claude must NEVER modify files in `db/migrations/`, (2) Claude should prefer custom logging over `console.log`, (3) All TypeScript files must be auto-formatted with Prettier after every edit. All three are in CLAUDE.md. Claude sometimes violates requirement 1. How should you restructure these requirements?

options.A: Add `Edit(/db/migrations/**)` to `permissions.deny` in project settings, keep logging preference in CLAUDE.md, add a PostToolUse hook for Prettier on TypeScript files
options.B: Move all three requirements into `.claude/rules/` as path-scoped rules
options.C: Rewrite all three in CLAUDE.md using stronger directive language and add a PreToolUse hook for TypeScript edits
options.D: Configure hooks for all three: PreToolUse to block db/migrations, PostToolUse to run Prettier

questions.explanation: Match requirement type to mechanism: hard prohibition → permissions.deny, behavioral preference → CLAUDE.md, automated action → PostToolUse hook.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Monorepo – @imports
questions.text: Your monorepo contains shared coding standards: `security-rules.md`, `api-design.md`, and a third. Your 15 packages each have different needs. Currently, package CLAUDE.md files duplicate all three standards, applying irrelevant guidance to each package. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Put all standards in the root CLAUDE.md with override instructions like "ignore security-rules.md when package does not handle user data"
options.B: Create a `shared-standards.md` that combines all three, have each package CLAUDE.md reference only relevant parts
options.C: Create `.claude/rules/` files for each standard with YAML frontmatter listing every relevant package directory
options.D: Use `@imports` in each package CLAUDE.md to reference only the specific standard files relevant to that package

questions.explanation: @imports in each package CLAUDE.md: clean, DRY, each package gets exactly what it needs. Standards live in one place — updates propagate automatically.

questions.title: [Real Exam] When to Use Plan Mode
questions.text: You are implementing a caching layer for API responses to speed up the `/products` endpoint. You have a rough idea — Redis with a 5-minute TTL — but you are not sure what other considerations a robust implementation requires. What is the most effective way to start your iterative workflow?

options.A: Start with a minimal request: "Add Redis caching to /products with 5-minute TTL." Add features and fix issues through follow-up prompts
options.B: Write a specification with your known requirements and "TBD" markers for uncertain areas
options.C: Use plan mode to analyze the current /products endpoint implementation, then provide your caching requirements before implementing
options.D: Ask Claude to interview you about the caching requirements before implementing

questions.explanation: You know what you want but not what you do not know. Plan mode on the existing endpoint surfaces cache invalidation, failure handling, and concurrency considerations before building.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Plan Mode vs Direct – Simple Change
questions.text: You need to add a date validation check ensuring event dates are in the future. This requires adding a conditional statement to one existing function in the reservation flow. What is the most efficient approach?

options.A: Use direct execution to make the change
options.B: Start with extended thinking mode enabled to ensure thorough reasoning about the validation logic
options.C: Enter plan mode first to create a detailed implementation strategy before making the change
options.D: Enter plan mode to analyze how the validation might impact other parts of the reservation flow

questions.explanation: One conditional in one function: clear, targeted, contained change. Direct execution — just implement it. Small + clear + contained = direct execution.

questions.title: [Real Exam] TDD for Iterative Refinement
questions.text: You are implementing a complex graph traversal algorithm with specific performance requirements and edge cases. You want to most effectively enable progressive improvement across multiple sessions with Claude. What approach will most effectively enable this?

options.A: Have Claude extensively research the algorithm and create a detailed implementation plan using extended thinking, then implement
options.B: Provide Claude with a reference implementation from documentation, then ask it to rewrite the code to match your codebase style
options.C: Provide Claude with a detailed natural language specification of the algorithm, including all requirements and edge cases
options.D: Write a test suite covering expected behavior, edge cases, and performance requirements before implementation — share test failures with each refinement request

questions.explanation: Tests are the perfect multi-session iterative refinement mechanism: same objective success criteria every session, specific failure messages enable targeted fixes, progress is measurable.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Interdependent Bug Fixes
questions.text: You asked Claude Code to build a PDF report generation feature. The initial output has three formatting issues: table columns too narrow, dates without proper formatting, and incorrect page break handling. These issues interact — changing column widths affects how dates display and content height affects page breaks. What is the most effective approach?

options.A: Provide all three issues in a single detailed message with exact specifications for each, allowing Claude to address them together
options.B: Address the column width issue first with specific measurements, verify it works, then fix date formatting within the corrected column structure
options.C: Start fresh with a detailed prompt specifying all formatting requirements upfront, rather than fixing the specific technical issues
options.D: Show Claude an example of a correctly formatted report and ask it to match that output

questions.explanation: Interdependent bugs should be fixed simultaneously. Column widths affect date display and page breaks — sequential fixes chase a moving target. Claude accounts for all interdependencies in one coherent update.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Architectural Decisions
questions.text: You are tasked with adding real-time updates to the application. This could be implemented using WebSockets, Server-Sent Events, or polling, each with different infrastructure requirements. What is the most effective way to begin this task?

options.A: Use direct execution to implement polling first, then evaluate whether to upgrade to WebSockets later
options.B: Start direct execution with WebSockets, then refactor if infrastructure issues arise
options.C: Enter plan mode to explore the architecture, evaluate trade-offs, and present options for team approval before implementing
options.D: Use direct execution with a prompt asking Claude to analyze all approaches and implement the one it determines is best

questions.explanation: Multiple valid architectural options with different infrastructure implications = plan mode + team approval. Humans must own architecture and infrastructure choices.

questions.title: [Real Exam] Slash Commands – Team Workflow
questions.text: Your team wants Claude to follow a detailed code review checklist (8 items) when reviewing pull requests — invoked when starting reviews. Claude is also used extensively for writing features, debugging, and generating documentation. Currently, developers manually paste the checklist at the start of each review session. Which approach best addresses this workflow need?

options.A: Create a `/review` slash command containing the checklist, invoked when starting reviews
options.B: Create a dedicated review subagent with the checklist embedded in its configuration
options.C: Configure plan mode as the default for code review sessions
options.D: Add the checklist to the project CLAUDE.md file under a "Code Review" section

questions.explanation: /review slash command = on-demand invocation. Developer types /review → checklist loaded → review begins. Does not pollute feature writing, debugging, or documentation sessions.
